programs.name: Toronto Bail Program (TBP)
programs.operator: John Howard Society of Toronto [VERIFY current operator]
programs.eligibility: Adults charged with criminal offences appearing at Toronto courts who are otherwise facing detention; generally excludes those charged with murder or certain firearms offences; must reside in or have ties to the Toronto area. [VERIFY current exclusions]
courthouses_served: Toronto Old City Hall (Ontario Court of Justice)
College Park (Ontario Court of Justice)
2201 Finch Ave W (Toronto North — Ontario Court of Justice)
1911 Eglinton Ave E (Scarborough — Ontario Court of Justice) [VERIFY]
1000 Finch Ave W (Toronto — Superior Court bail reviews) [VERIFY]
programs.program_features: Community supervision as alternative to surety or detention; regular in-person reporting (frequency set by bail order, typically 1–3 times per week); curfew verification via phone or in-person checks; referrals to treatment, housing, and employment services; drug and alcohol counselling referrals where applicable. [VERIFY current reporting cadence and curfew-check method]
programs.how_to_propose: Raise at bail hearing when Crown or justice is considering surety or detention. Request brief recess for same-day intake interview by TBP staff present at courthouse. TBP staff prepare a supervision plan for the court. Counsel should confirm TBP intake staff are present before the hearing or call TBP office that morning. Provide TBP with client's residential address, contact info, and summary of charges. [VERIFY intake hours — typically 8:30 AM to courthouse close on business days]
programs.url_or_locator: [VERIFY — historically https://johnhoward.on.ca/toronto/ or contact Toronto courthouse duty counsel office]

programs.name: John Howard Society of Peel-Halton-Dufferin — Bail Verification and Supervision Program
programs.eligibility: Adults appearing at Brampton or Milton courthouses charged with criminal offences who are candidates for release but lack a surety; residency in Peel, Halton, or Dufferin regions preferred. [VERIFY charge-type exclusions]
courthouses_served: A. Grenville and William Davis Courthouse (Brampton)
Milton Ontario Court of Justice [VERIFY]
programs.program_features: Bail verification (confirming address, employment, community ties for the court), ongoing supervision with scheduled reporting, referrals to community services including addiction and mental health support. [VERIFY supervision intensity and reporting frequency]
programs.how_to_propose: Contact JHS Peel office or locate their intake worker at the Brampton courthouse before the bail hearing. Worker conducts same-day interview and provides a bail supervision plan to duty counsel or retained counsel. Counsel presents plan to justice as an alternative to surety under rung 5 of the Antic ladder. [VERIFY intake worker hours at courthouse]
programs.url_or_locator: [VERIFY — https://johnhowardphd.com/ or contact Brampton courthouse duty counsel]

programs.name: Ottawa Bail Verification and Supervision Program
programs.operator: John Howard Society of Ottawa [VERIFY — may also involve Salvation Army Ottawa or other community partner]
programs.eligibility: Adults appearing at Ottawa courthouse who would otherwise be detained or require a surety; must have Ottawa-area residence or ties. [VERIFY specific charge exclusions and whether youth stream exists]
courthouses_served: Ottawa Courthouse (161 Elgin Street — Ontario Court of Justice)
[VERIFY whether Courthouse at 1 Nicholas Street is also served]
programs.program_features: Bail verification (community ties, address, employment), supervision with regular reporting, referrals to community agencies for substance abuse, mental health, housing. [VERIFY reporting cadence]
programs.how_to_propose: Request intake interview through JHS Ottawa staff at courthouse or by calling the JHS Ottawa office before the hearing date. Same-day interviews are generally available during business hours. Counsel should flag the program to the Crown and justice at first opportunity. [VERIFY intake availability on weekends for after-hours arrests]
programs.url_or_locator: [VERIFY — https://www.jhsottawa.ca/ or contact Ottawa duty counsel office]

courthouses_served: John Dickey Memorial Courthouse (Hamilton — Ontario Court of Justice) [VERIFY courthouse name and address]
programs.program_features: Bail verification, supervision with regular reporting, referrals to community support services. [VERIFY specifics]

programs.url_or_locator: [VERIFY — https://www.jhshamilton.on.ca/ or contact Hamilton courthouse duty counsel]

programs.name: BC Bail Supervision Program
programs.operator: BC Community Corrections (Ministry of Public Safety and Solicitor General); in some regions contracted to John Howard Society of BC or Elizabeth Fry Society [VERIFY current operator and contracting structure]
programs.eligibility: Adults released on bail where the court orders bail supervision as a condition of release; available across BC courthouses in principle, though intensity and availability vary by region. Not charge-specific but more commonly ordered for moderate-to-serious offences where detention is the alternative. [VERIFY current eligibility criteria and regional gaps]
courthouses_served: Vancouver Provincial Court (222 Main Street)
Surrey Provincial Court
Victoria Provincial Court (850 Burdett Avenue) [VERIFY address]
Kelowna Law Courts
Available province-wide in theory; rural availability is inconsistent [VERIFY]
programs.program_features: Regular reporting to a bail supervisor (frequency set by court order, typically weekly to bi-weekly); curfew checks; referrals to substance use, mental health, and housing services; electronic monitoring may be available in some cases as an add-on [VERIFY EM availability]. Supervision intensity is risk-based. [VERIFY current reporting standards]
programs.how_to_propose: Counsel proposes bail supervision as a release condition at the bail hearing. Unlike Ontario, there is generally no same-day intake interview at the courthouse; the court orders supervision and the accused reports to Community Corrections post-release. Counsel should confirm the local Community Corrections office location and hours, and propose a reporting deadline (e.g., within 24 hours of release). [VERIFY whether any courthouse has on-site intake]
programs.url_or_locator: [VERIFY — check BC Corrections website or contact local Community Corrections office]

programs.name: Vancouver Drug Treatment Court — Bail-Stage Referral Stream
programs.operator: Vancouver Drug Treatment Court (operated under the Provincial Court of BC with support from Vancouver Coastal Health) [VERIFY]
programs.eligibility: Adults whose offences are substantially connected to substance use; must be willing to participate in treatment; generally excludes offences involving serious violence or trafficking for profit. Residency in Vancouver area. [VERIFY current eligibility criteria — DTC may have shifted scope]
courthouses_served: Vancouver Provincial Court (222 Main Street)
programs.program_features: Intensive judicial supervision with frequent court appearances; mandatory substance use treatment; regular drug testing; graduated sanctions and incentives; housing support. Participation can begin at bail stage with the accused entering the DTC stream as a condition of release. [VERIFY whether bail-stage entry is still available or only post-plea]
programs.how_to_propose: Counsel contacts DTC coordinator at Vancouver Provincial Court to arrange screening. Raise DTC as a bail plan alternative to detention, framing it as a structured supervision plan. Be prepared that screening may take 1–2 days; request a brief remand for assessment if needed. [VERIFY DTC coordinator contact and screening timeline]
programs.url_or_locator: [VERIFY — https://www.provincialcourt.bc.ca/about-the-court/court-innovation/drug-treatment-court or contact Vancouver Provincial Court registry]

programs.name: Indigenous Courts and First Nations Courts — Bail-Relevant Streams [VERIFY program names]
programs.operator: Various: New Westminster Indigenous Court, Duncan First Nations Court [VERIFY — other locations may exist including Kamloops and Prince George]
programs.eligibility: Self-identified Indigenous accused; court applies Gladue principles at bail per R. v. Gladue and R. v. Ipeelee. Some courts have dedicated Gladue caseworkers available at bail stage. [VERIFY which courts have active Gladue bail streams vs. sentencing-only]
courthouses_served: New Westminster Law Courts (Indigenous Court) [VERIFY]
Duncan Provincial Court (First Nations Court) [VERIFY]
[VERIFY other locations]
programs.program_features: Culturally appropriate bail supervision plans incorporating Indigenous community supports, Elder involvement, healing plans; may connect accused to band-level supervision or Indigenous-run residential programs as surety alternatives. [VERIFY specifics]
programs.how_to_propose: Request transfer to Indigenous Court stream or request that Gladue factors be considered at bail. If a Gladue report is not available, counsel can provide an oral Gladue submission or request a brief remand for Gladue caseworker involvement. Flag Indigenous identity at earliest opportunity (ideally before bail hearing). [VERIFY Gladue caseworker availability at specific courthouses]
programs.url_or_locator: [VERIFY — contact Provincial Court of BC or local Indigenous Justice Centre]

programs.operator: Provincial Court of BC with health authority partnerships (Vancouver Coastal Health in Vancouver, others regionally) [VERIFY]

programs.how_to_propose: Counsel raises mental health as relevant at bail, requests psychiatric assessment or connection to forensic liaison worker at courthouse. In Vancouver, contact the Court Liaison Program through Vancouver Coastal Health. [VERIFY contact details and whether same-day assessment is available]

general_notes: Programs are alternatives to a surety; counsel should canvass them at rung 5 of the Antic ladder (R. v. Antic, 2017 SCC 27) before agreeing to a personal surety.
Eligibility and availability are local and change frequently — confirm with the courthouse duty counsel office or program intake worker on the day of the hearing.
Some programs (particularly in Ontario) require a pre-bail interview the same day; flag to the court early and request a brief recess or remand if intake is pending.
In BC, bail supervision is typically ordered as a condition rather than arranged via same-day courthouse intake; counsel should confirm local Community Corrections availability.

For Indigenous accused, Gladue principles apply at bail (R. v. Gladue, [1999] 1 SCR 688; R. v. Ipeelee, 2012 SCC 13). Counsel must raise these factors affirmatively; the court's failure to consider them is an error of law.
counsel_practice_tips: Antic ladder: The SCC in R. v. Antic, 2017 SCC 27, established that release at the earliest opportunity on the least onerous conditions is the default. Bail supervision programs sit below surety on the ladder. Always propose a program before conceding a surety. [VERIFY — confirm CLA/CBA Criminal Justice Section best-practice guidance on program proposals]
Always confirm program intake hours before the hearing day. If the hearing is after hours or on a weekend, propose program enrolment as a release condition with intake the next business day.

Where both surety AND a program are proposed, frame the program as the lesser-restrictive default per Antic; surety should be the fallback only if the program is insufficient in the Crown's or court's view.

For Indigenous accused: prepare an oral Gladue submission for bail even if a written Gladue report is not available. Identify the accused's Indigenous community, any history of residential schools (personal or intergenerational), and community supports available. A Gladue caseworker or Indigenous courtworker, where available, can assist with this. See R. v. Ipeelee, 2012 SCC 13 at paras 59–60 and 77–87.
Document the program's supervision plan in writing and file it with the court. Justices are more receptive to structured, specific plans than vague oral references to 'community supervision.'

If a program rejects the client (e.g., due to charge type), document the rejection for the record — it may support an argument that detention is disproportionate where no lesser alternative is available.
[VERIFY] Whether Legal Aid Ontario or Legal Aid BC cover costs associated with bail supervision program intake or Gladue caseworker reports at bail stage.
